coredata.dc:title: A novel antibacterial agent based on AgNPs and Fe3O4 loaded chitin microspheres with peroxidase-like activity for synergistic antibacterial activity and wound-healing
coredata.dc:creator: Yu, Ningxiang
Cai, Taimei
Sun, Yong
Jiang, Chengjia
Xiong, Hua
Li, Yanbin
Peng, Hailong
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a novel antibacterial agent was developed based on chitin nanofibrous microspheres loaded with AgNPs and Fe3O4 nanoparticles (Ag-Fe3O4-NMs) for synergistic antibacterial activity and wound healing. Ag-Fe3O4-NMs was prepared via an in situ synthetic method which showed an excellent porosity and wettability. Moreover, Ag-Fe3O4-NMs were capable of sustained release of Ag+ and catalysed the decomposition of low H2O2 concentrations to generate hydroxyl radical (OH). The OH and Ag+ showed higher antibacterial activity and inhibited the toxicity with high dose of AgNPs and H2O2. In vitro biocompatibility results suggested that Ag-Fe3O4-NMs have low toxicity and low hemolysis. Thus, a novel antibacterial agent with enhanced synergistic antibacterial activity was obtained by combination of Ag-Fe3O4-NMs and H2O2 at a low and biologlically safe dosage, which could facilitate fibroblast growth, accelerate epithelialization, and promote the healing rate of infected wounds.
coredata.dcterms:subject: AgNPs
Fe3O4
Chitin nanofibrous microsphere
Peroxidase-like activity
Synergetic antibacterial effect
Wound healing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Peroxidase-like catalytic activity and kinetic assay of Ag-Fe3O4-NMs
Morphology and fluorescence microscopy of bacteria
In vivo rat wound model and healing process
Designing an antibacterial agent for synergistic antibacterial activity and wound-healing
Ag release from Ag-Fe3O4-NMs and Ag-Fe3O4-NMs + H2O2 in PBS
Antibacterial assessment of Ag-Fe3O4-NMs and synergistic antibacterial activity

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of Fe3O4-NMs (A, C, E) and Ag-Fe3O4-NMs (B, D, F), respectively; and EDX analysis of Fe3O4-NMs (G) and Ag-Fe3O4-NMs (H), respectively; N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm of Fe3O4-NMs and Ag-Fe3O4-NMs (I).

Magnetization curves of Fe3O4-NMs and Ag-Fe3O4-NMs (A); XRD pattern (B) of Fe3O4, chitin, Fe3O4-NMs, and Ag-Fe3O4-NMs; Full-scale XPS spectra of chitin, Fe3O4-NMs, and Ag-Fe3O4-NMs (C), XPS spectra of Fe (D), Ag (E), elements; Cumulative release profiles of Silver in PBS from Ag-Fe3O4-NMs and Ag-Fe3O4-NMs + H2O2 (F).

(A) Time-dependent absorbance changes at 652 nm with TMB solution after treatment with control, H2O2 (20 mM), Ag-Fe3O4-NMs (150 μg/mL), and Ag-Fe3O4-NMs (150 μg/mL) + H2O2 (20 mM), respectively. (B) Temperature and pH dependent peroxidase-like activity of Ag-Fe3O4-NMs (25 μg/mL) with H2O2 (10 mM); (C) Time-dependent absorbance changes at 652 nm of TMB solution after treatment with different concentrations of Ag-Fe3O4-NMs (20, 40, 60, and 80 μg/mL) in NaAC solution (pH 6.0, 25 mM, 25 °C) with H2O2 (2 mM); (D) Time-dependent absorbance changes at 652 nm of TMB solution with different concentration of H2O2 (10 μM, 100 μM, 1 mM, and 10 mM) after treatment with Ag-Fe3O4-NMs (100 μg/mL) in NaAC solution (pH 6.0, 25 mM, 25 °C).

Photographs of bacterial colonies after treatment with Fe3O4, Fe3O4-NMs, and Ag-Fe3O4-NMs for 2 h at concentration of 100 μg/mL (A); Survival rate of E. coli at 105 CFU/mL and S. aureus at 105 CFU/mL after treatment with Ag-Fe3O4-NMs at different time and concentration (B); Survival rate of E. coli at 105 CFU/mL and S. aureus at 105 CFU/mL incubated with different concentrations of H2O2 after treatment with or without Ag-Fe3O4-NMs (6.25 μg/mL) (C).

(A) SEM images of E. coli and S. aureus treated with (a) and (c) PBS; (b) and (d) Ag-Fe3O4-NMs; (e) and (g) H2O2; (f) and (g) Ag-Fe3O4-NMs + H2O2; (B) Fluorescence microscopy observation images of E. coli and S. aureus treated with (b) and (e) PBS; (c) and (f) Ag-Fe3O4-NMs; (g) and (i) H2O2; (h) and (j) Ag-Fe3O4-NMs + H2O2; and corresponding optical of E. coli and S. aureus treated with (a) and (d) PBS.
Schematic illustration of the possible synergetic antibacterial mechanism of Ag-Fe3O4-NMs with H2O2.

Cell viability (A) of Fe3O4-NMs and Ag-Fe3O4-NMs after 24 h incubation at different concentration (12.5, 25.0, and 50.0 µg/mL), and Fluorescence microscopy observation images of cell treated with different of Ag-Fe3O4-NMs (B); photographs of rabbit blood (C) and hemolysis ratio (D) after treatment with Fe3O4-NMs and Ag-Fe3O4-NMs. (a, b, c, d, and e is 50 μg/mL, 25 μg/mL, 12.5 μg/mL, water, and saline).

In vivo assessments of different groups for wound healing infected by E. coli at days of 1, 4, 7, 11 and 15 (A); wound disinfection model before and after treated with Ag-Fe3O4-NMs + H2O2 dressing (B); The wound area closure at different time (C); The histological images of the skin tissues on wounds with H&E staining. (a: control group; b: H2O2 group; c: Ag-Fe3O4-NMs group; d: Ag-Fe3O4-NMs + H2O2 group) (D).
Schematic illustraion of preparation Ag-Fe3O4-NMs and designing of synergetic antibacterial system.

head.ce:title: A novel antibacterial agent based on AgNPs and Fe3O4 loaded chitin microspheres with peroxidase-like activity for synergistic antibacterial activity and wound-healing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Technology, Nanchang University, No. 235 Nanjing East Road, Nanchang 330047, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Resources, Environmental, and Chemical Engineering, Nanchang University, No. 999 Xuefu Avenue, Nanchang 330031, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Resources, Environmental, and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biological and Agricultural Engineering, University of Arkansas, Fayetteville, AR 72701, USA

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Biological and Agricultural Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Technology, Nanchang University, No. 235 Nanjing East Road, Nanchang 330047, China (H. Peng).

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a novel antibacterial agent was developed based on chitin nanofibrous microspheres loaded with AgNPs and Fe3O4 nanoparticles (Ag-Fe3O4-NMs) for synergistic antibacterial activity and wound healing. Ag-Fe3O4-NMs was prepared via an in situ synthetic method which showed an excellent porosity and wettability. Moreover, Ag-Fe3O4-NMs were capable of sustained release of Ag+ and catalysed the decomposition of low H2O2 concentrations to generate hydroxyl radical (OH). The OH and Ag+ showed higher antibacterial activity and inhibited the toxicity with high dose of AgNPs and H2O2. In vitro biocompatibility results suggested that Ag-Fe3O4-NMs have low toxicity and low hemolysis. Thus, a novel antibacterial agent with enhanced synergistic antibacterial activity was obtained by combination of Ag-Fe3O4-NMs and H2O2 at a low and biologlically safe dosage, which could facilitate fibroblast growth, accelerate epithelialization, and promote the healing rate of infected wounds.

ce:section.ce:para: Antibiotics have been remarkably successful in treating infectious diseases caused by bacteria, and have dramatically decreased mortality over the course of history (Singh et al., 2000). Unfortunately, due to the frequent and irrational use of antibiotics, the rapid emergence of antibiotic-resistant bacteria has become a new threat to human health (Huang et al., 2016). Therefore, exploring new antibacterial agents with favorable safety and potent antibacterial activity that will not engender bacterial resistance is necessary (Huh and Kwon, 2011; Hemaiswarya et al., 2008). Various nanomaterials, such as carbon nanomaterials (Tian et al., 2014; Deng et al., 2014; Zhan et al., 2015), metal oxide nanoparticles (Chen et al., 2010; Schwartz et al., 2012; Huang et al., 2009), organic polymers (Sémiramoth et al., 2012; Kang et al., 2015), and inorganic nanoparticles (Mei et al., 2013; Su et al., 2009; Travan et al., 2009), have potential for defeating the drug-resistant bacteria. However, the inherent toxicity of these nanomaterials at high dosage limits their use as antibacterial agents. Moreover, for many superbug infections, monotherapy is becoming increasing inadequate (Cohen, 2000; Walsh, 2000; Chen et al., 2013). The combination of multiple antibacterial therapeutic modalities is a promising approach to decrease the dosage and side effects of antibacterial agents, enhance antibacterial efficiency, and induce the potential synergistic therapeutic effect (Tian et al., 2017). Thus, the development of an effective combination therapy is crucial for treating bacterial infections.

Silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) has been considered as excellent antibacterial agent for inhibition of bacterial infection and wound treatment due to its broad-spectrum antibacterial activity and limited bacterial resistance (Yu et al., 2017; Cheng et al., 2013). Recently, AgNPs combined with other antibiotics such as tetracycline, gentamicin, and penicillin have shown strong synergistic antimicrobial activity against gram-negative and gram-positive bacteria, even against drug-resistant bacteria (Zhou et al., 2017). However, these antibiotic based antibacterial agents are cost intensive and may promote bacterial resistance to antibiotics. Thus, the development of efficient silver based combination therapy using inexpensive and difficult to resist materials is highly desirable (Huang et al., 2017). Moreover, H2O2 has been widely used as a therapeutic reagent in wound disinfection. However, the therapeutic concentration of H2O2 traditionally used to disinfect is high (usually 0.5–3%, ca. 166 mM to 1 M), which are harmful to healthy tissues and prevents wound healing (Sun et al., 2014). The introduction of a nanocatalyst such as Fe3O4, Au/g-C3N4, MoS2, and carbon-based nanomaterials to catalyze low concentrated H2O2 to generate hydroxyl radicals (OH), which have higher antibacterial activity than H2O2, are popular for antibacterial applications (Gao et al., 2014; Wang et al., 2017; Yin et al., 2016; Sun et al., 2014). Moreover, among these nanocatalysts, Fe3O4 is one of the most popular peroxidase-like that assists H2O2 in antibacterial applications at a bio-safety dose because of its biocompatibility and easy separation property (Gao et al., 2014). However, Fe3O4 has poor antibacterial activity and inherent toxicity at high dosage. In this study, with the purpose of enhancing the antibacterial activity and reducing the dosage of catalyst and H2O2, a synergistic antimicrobial agent is prepared combination of AgNPs with H2O2 using Fe3O4 as nanocatalyst. However, conventional AgNPs are disadvantageous because of their easy aggregation, poor bioavailability, and high environmental toxicity or biological hazard (Rizzello and Pompa, 2014; Dos Santos et al., 2014). Thus, the development of relatively green synthetic processes using environmentally friendly solvents and renewable, nontoxic starting materials and reagents is highly desirable.

According to the concept of sustainable development, many researchers have applied natural polymers as reducing and stabilizing agents to prepare AgNPs by in situ one-pot syntheses, such as starch (Ghaseminezhad et al., 2012), chitosan (Sanpui et al., 2008), silkworms (Yu et al., 2017), cellulose (Cheng et al., 2013), and chitin (Guo et al., 2016). Such methods can decrease biotoxicity, avoid aggregation, and render the long-lasting antibacterial effect of AgNPs. Among these natural polymers, chitin is the most abundant renewable polysaccharide and has attracted tremendous attention due to its nontoxic, biocompatible, mucoadhesive, and biodegradable properties (Fan et al., 2008). Recently, nanofibrous microspheres (NMs) has been successfully fabricated from chitin nanofibers via a sol-gel process (Shen et al., 2016). Moreover, with the 3D-nanoporous structure, high surface area, and abundant acetylamino groups of NMs, AgNPs and Fe3O4 have been successfully loaded into it for rapid hydrogenation catalysis, and the results showed that AgNP-Fe3O4 nanocomposites have not only magnetic properties, but also catalytic activity (Duan et al., 2014; Duan et al., 2015). Moreover, the porous structure of NMs can slowly release AgNPs and prevent their aggregation. These studies are expected to have a more useful application in the antibacterial field than conventional AgNPs. However, to the best our knowledge, the use of Ag-Fe3O4-NMs in combination with H2O2 to design an antibacterial agent has not been reported.

Therefore, in this study, chitin was used to construct magnetic-nanofibrous microspheres (Fe3O4-NMs) through the low-cost and energy-efficient pathway of the sol-gel transition method. Subsequently, Fe3O4-NMs were used to immobilize AgNPs by an in situ one-pot and green synthetic route. This two-step synthetic method is not much different from previous one-step method to prepare Ag-Fe3O4-NMs, which enable the small sized AgNPs and Fe3O4 to rapidly disperse in the 3D-porous framework of NMs. NMs can effectively avoid the aggregation of AgNPs and ensure the continuous release of AgNPs, inducing long-term antibacterial activity. The intrinsic peroxidase-like catalytic activity of AgNPs and Fe3O4 strengthens the synergistic antibacterial activity at safe dose of the combination of Ag-Fe3O4-NMs with H2O2. The sustainable source of chitin, environmentally friendly processes, and enhanced synergistic antibacterial activity indicated that Ag-Fe3O4-NMs may be a potential antibacterial agent to help recover from infectious diseases caused by bacterial.

Chitin was obtained from Golden-Shell Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Zhejiang, China) and purified according to previous report (Duan et al., 2015). Escherichia coli (E. coli, strain ATCC 25922) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus, strain ATCC 25923) were obtained from Guangdong Huankai Microbial Sci.&Tech.Co.,Ltd. Hexahydrate ferric chloride, trisodium citrate, ethylene glycol, sodium acetate, hydrochloric acid, sodium hydrate, urea, and silver nitrate were purchased from Xilong Chemical Co., Ltd. (Shantou, Guangdong, China). Span 85, Tween 85, and sodium chlorite were obtained from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Other chemical reagents were obtained Sigma-Aldrich. All chemicals used in this work were of commercial analytical grade. Ultrapure water used throughout the work was produced by a Milli-Q Ultrapure water system (Millipore, Bedford, USA).

The Fe3O4-NMs was prepared according to previous report (Duan et al., 2015). Briefly, the purified chitin powder (5 g) was dispersed into a mixed solution containing NaOH, urea and deionized water with a weight ratio of 8:4:88. Subsequently, a transparent chitin solution was obtained using a freezing-thawing process. Fe3O4 nanoparticles (0.4 g) (Fig. S1) were dispersed into water (5 mL) by ultrasonication for 5 min and then mixed with the chitin solution at 0 °C. Span 85 (1.1 g) and isooctane (50 g) were added into a round-bottom flask at 0 °C and stirring (1000 rpm) for 30 min. After that, the Fe3O4 and chitin mixed solution was injected into the flask with stirring for 60 min at 0 °C. And then, Towen 85 (0.6 g) and isooctane (3.5 g) were added into the mixed suspension and stirred for another 60 min to achieve stable water/oil emulsion droplets, and then transferred to a bath (60 °C) for 10 min to form Fe3O4-NMs. Finally, Fe3O4-NMs suspension was separated using a magnet and washed with distilled water, ethanol, and then t-BuOH for three times. The obtained Fe3O4-NMs were lyophilized for further use.

Ag-Fe3O4-NMs were fabricated by an in situ synthesis previous method with minor modification, according to a previous study, with minor modification (Guo et al., 2016). Fe3O4-NMs (25 mg) were dispersed into AgNO3 aqueous solution (12 mL, 8 mM) and stirred for 24 h. Subsequently, trisodium citrate aqueous solution (0.8 mL, 50 mM) was added and stirred at 80 °C for approximately 1.5 h unitl the suspension change from yellow to black-brown. After that, the Ag-Fe3O4-NMs were separated using a magnet and washed with pure water three times.

The morphology of Ag-Fe3O4-NMs and bacterial were observed by scanning electron microscope (SEM, JSM 5600LV, operating at 20 kV). The morphology of Fe3O4 was obtained from transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2010HR, Japan). The low angel X-ray diffractometer (XRD) patterns were recorded using XRD analyzer (D8-FOCUS, Bruker, Karlsruhe, Germany). The magnetic properties were examined using vibrating specimen magnetometer (VSM) (7407, Lake Shore Cryotronics, Westerville, OH, USA). N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms were obtained with Beishide instruments (3H-2000, Beijing, China). An X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectrum was evaluated using an ESCALAB apparatus (Omicrometer, Taunusstein, Germany). The fluorescence microscope images were obtained from an LSM 710 confocal laser scanning fluorescence microscope (Carl Zeiss, Oberkochen, Germany). Zeta potential was measured using a Malvern Zeta/sizer Nano-ZSE. Contact angles were measured using a contact angle System OCA25 (Dataphysics, Germany). The swelling ratio of the samples were measured by the gravimetric method and calculated using the following formula: Swelling ratio = (mw − md)/md, where mw and md are wet and dry weight of the samples

In vitro release of Ag+ from Ag-Fe3O4-NMs (50 mg) and Ag-Fe3O4-NMs (50 mg) + H2O2 (1 mL, 10−3 M) were investigated in a dialysis tubes (MWCO 3.5 kDa) containing 10 mL water. The dialysis tubes were immersed in 1000 mL phosphate-buffered solution (PBS, pH = 7.0) as release media. The dialysis was carried out at 37 °C with shaking (50 rpm). At specified time intervals, 2 mL of dispersions outside the dialysis tubes were taken out, and the amounts of Ag released from Ag-Fe3O4-NMs and Ag-Fe3O4-NMs + H2O2 were analysed using ICP-MS (Kittler et al., 2010).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Peroxidase-like catalytic activity and kinetic assay of Ag-Fe3O4-NMs
ce:section.ce:para: The peroxidase-like activity and steady state kinetic of Ag-Fe3O4-NMs were investigated via the catalytic oxidation of the peroxidase substrate TMB in the presence of H2O2. Ag-Fe3O4-NMs (50 μg/mL) and TMB (1 mM) were added into NaAC solution (pH 6.0, 25 mM, 1 mL) at 37 °C in the presence of H2O2 with the concentration changing from 10 μM to 10 mM. The absorbance of TMB was measured using UV spectra at 652 nm. The Michaelis-Menten constant was obtained by the plot linear-fitting method: 1/v = (Km/Vmax) × (1/C) + 1/Vmax, where v is the initial velocity, Vmax is the maximal reaction velocity, C is the concentration of substrate, and Km is the Michaelis constant.

Escherichia coli (E. coli, strain ATCC 25922) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus, strain ATCC 25923) were used as the model bacteria. Two bacteria were grown in Luria-Bertani (LB) medium (yeast extract 5 g, tryptone 10 g, and NaCl 5 g in 1 L of DI water, with the pH adjusted to 7.0 with NaOH) under agitation (180 rpm) at 37 °C for 24 h, the bacterial content of the suspension was approximately 108 colony forming units (CFU/mL). Then, the cultures were harvested by centrifugation (4000 rpm) for 10 min and washed three times with sterile DI water. And then, the bacterial suspensions were diluted to contain 105 CFU/mL cells using sterile phosphate-buffered saline (PBS, 0.01 mM, pH 7.0).

The antibacterial activities of H2O2, Ag-Fe3O4-NMs, and Ag-Fe3O4-NMs + H2O2 were evaluated on LB agar culture plate using the plate counting method. Different concentrations of H2O2 (50 μL), Ag-Fe3O4-NMs (50 μL), and Ag-Fe3O4-NMs + H2O2 were placed into a bacterial suspension (2 mL) of both E. coli and S. aureus, respectively, and incubated at 37 °C for 2 h with shaking (180 rpm). Then, the resulting mixtures (50 μL) were pipetted out and dispersed onto LB agar plates. Bacterial colonies on culture plates were counted after incubation at 37 °C for 24 h. Time (15, 60, and 120 min) and concentration of H2O2 (1.0 × 10−8, 1.0 × 10−7, 1.0 × 10−6, 1.0 × 10−5, 1.0 × 10−4, 1.0 × 10−3, 1.0 × 10−2, 1.0 × 10−1, and 1.0 mol L−1), and Ag-Fe3O4-NMs (6.25, 12.5, 25, 50, and 100 μg/mL) kill assays were performed using the plate counting method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and fluorescence microscopy of bacteria
ce:section.ce:para: Samples of E. coli and S. aureus treated with PBS, H2O2 (10−3 M), Ag-Fe3O4-NMs (50 μg/mL), and Ag-Fe3O4-NMs + H2O2 for 30 min were fixed using the glass coverslips with glutaraldehyde solution (2.5%) for 2.5 h at 4 °C. Subsequently, the samples were dehydrated in a series of ethanol solutions (30, 50, 70, 90, and 100%) for 20 min. The samples were treated with lyophilization and then observed using SEM. Meanwhile, the bacterial suspension (8 mL, 105 CFU/mL) was incubated with PBS, H2O2 (10-3 M), Ag-Fe3O4-NMs (50 μg/mL), and Ag-Fe3O4-NMs + H2O2 with shaking (180 rpm) at 37 °C for 30 min, respectively. Then, 5 μL of bacterial suspensions were incubated with PI (20 μL, 10 μM) at 25 °C for 20 min in the dark and imaged using a fluorescence microscope.

Human skin fibroblasts (CCC-HSF-1) were used to determine Ag-Fe3O4-NMs cytotoxicity, and the Ag-Fe3O4-NMs was sterilized under a UV light for 30 min before the cytotoxicity experiment. Cells were seeded on 96-well plates in high-glucose Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (H/DMEM). After incubation for 24 h at 37 °C in a 5% CO2 humidified atmosphere, the cells (1 × 105) were treated with various concentrations of Ag-Fe3O4-NMs (0, 12.5, 25, and 50 μg/mL) and further incubated for 24 h at 37 °C. The medium was taken out, and the cells were washed with PBS solution. MTT (10 μL/well) in PBS solution (5 mg/mL) were added to each well and incubated at 37 °C for further 4 h. Then, the culture medium containing MTT was removed and the cell in wall was dissolved into DMSO. The absorbance was determined using a microplate reader at 490 nm, and the cell viability was calculated using the following formula: Cell viability (%) = (Absorbance of treatment/Absorbance of control) × 100%. Meanwhile, 10 μL of cell suspension, which was treated with Ag-Fe3O4-NMs, were incubated with PI (20 μL, 10 μM) at 25 °C for 30 min in the dark and visualized using a fluorescence microscope.

To investigate the biocompatibility of Ag-Fe3O4-NMs, hemolysis assay was performed using fresh rabbit blood (female New Zealand rabbit, 20 weeks of age, obtained from Jiangxi University of Traditional Chinese Medicine). The erythrocytes were collected from blood sample with centrifugation at 2000 rpm for 15 min and washed with saline. The erythrocytes dispersion were obtained by mixing erythrocytes into saline. Ag-Fe3O4-NMs with different concentration (500 μL) were added into erythrocytes dispersion (5 mL) and incubated at 37 °C for 2 h. After that, the undamaged erythrocytes were removed by centrifugation at 2000 rpm for 15 min, and the amount of hemoglobins in the supernatant were measured by UV at 540 nm. Saline and pure water were used as the negative and positive control, respectively. The percentage of hemolysis was calculated by the following equation:
Hemolysis
%
=
A 1
-
A 2
/
A 3
-
A 2
× 100 %
where A1, A2, and A3 was the absorbance of Ag-Fe3O4-NMs sample, negative control, and positive control, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo rat wound model and healing process
ce:section.ce:para: In vivo animal study was approved by the experimental animal science and technology center of Jiangxi University of Traditional Chinese Medicine, and all animal procedures were conducted in compliance with the Principles of Laboratory Animal Care of the National Institutes of Health, China. To evaluate the antibacterial effect of Ag-Fe3O4-NMs in vivo, Sprague–Dawley (SD) female rats aged at 8 weeks was used with the mass of 32 (eight rats in each group). The wound models (diameter of 15 mm) were built by removing the dorsal flank skin on the back of the rat (180–220 g). And then, E. coli (200 μL, 1 × 106 CFU/mL) was injected into the wound to build the infection model. Subsequently, the rats were randomly divided into four groups (eight parallel rat in each group): control group, Ag-Fe3O4-NMs group, H2O2 group, and Ag-Fe3O4-NMs + H2O2 group. The dressing were changed every day, and the wounds were photographed on days of 1, 4, 7, 11, and 15. The wound areas were measured using IPP 6.0 software. The healing ratio (HR) was calculated by the following formula: HR = (Ao − A)/Ao × 100%, in which Ao represents the number of pixels of the initial wound area, and A represents the number of pixels of the remaining wound area at the determined time. Moreover, the bacterial samples in each group from the wound area on day of 4, day 7, and day 11 were collected using sterile swabs and spread onto 1 mL of sterilized saline water. Subsequently, 100 μL of the suspension was spread on LB an agar plate and incubated at 37 °C for 1 day to check the antibacterial activity in vivo. For histological examination, the rats were sacrificed on the 7th and 15th days. The Hematoxylin and erosin (H&E) staining and immunohistochemistry staining were used for the histological analysis of wound tissues (Liu et al., 2017).

All data were evaluated as the mean ± standard deviation, and the results were contrasted via ANOVA. Results with P values of less than 0.05 were considered statistically significant. (*represents p < 0.05, **represents p < 0.01, and ***represents p < 0.001).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Designing an antibacterial agent for synergistic antibacterial activity and wound-healing
ce:section.ce:para: Scheme 1 illustrates the preparation of the antibacterial agent for synergistic antibacterial activity and wound-healing. Briefly, chitin was dissolved in NaOH-urea solution via a freezing-thawing method, and then Fe3O4-NMs was easily formed by thermally inducing self-assembly (Duan et al., 2015). Due to the presence of acetylamino groups of Fe3O4-NMs, AgNPs have been successfully immobilized on Fe3O4-NMs via an in situ synthesis method to obtain a 3D-porous structure for Ag-Fe3O4-NMs (Guo et al., 2016). Importantly, attributed to the intrinsic peroxidase-like catalytic activity of AgNPs and Fe3O4, a novel antibacterial agent has been successfully designed that has a strong synergistic antibacterial effect for the combination of Ag-Fe3O4-NMs with H2O2 at a low and biologically safe dose. Finally, a dressing based on Ag-Fe3O4-NMs with the H2O2 antibacterial system was designed and used in wound disinfection in vivo to facilitate biofilm formation and destruction of the E. coli. In this study, AgNPs and Fe3O4 were successfully loaded into the renewable NMs to enhance biocompatibility and avoid cell toxicity. Meanwhile, the remarkable synergetic antibacterial activity could effectively decrease the dose of Ag-Fe3O4-NMs and H2O2 when used as an antibacterial material. Moreover, the chitin nanofibrous porous structure of Ag-Fe3O4-NMs confers a superior surface wettability, which helps maintain a moist environment at the wound interface to accelerate wound healing (Lu et al., 2017).

The morphology of Fe3O4-NMs and Ag-Fe3O4-NMs was observed using SEM (Fig. 1 ). It is observed that Fe3O4-NMs (Fig. 1A, C, E) and Ag-Fe3O4-NMs (Fig. 1B, D, F) displayed a spherical shape and 3D-nanoporous architecture with interconnecting pores, which resulted from phase separation in the gelation of the chitin drop during the sol-gel process (Duan et al., 2015). Obviously, the spherical shape and size distribution of Ag-Fe3O4-NMs did not show an essential change during the growth of AgNPs compared with Fe3O4-NMs (inset in Fig. 1A and B by statistical analysis on at least 100 NMs), which due to AgNPs were synthesized in situ in the pores of Fe3O4-NMs (Guo et al., 2016). Fig. 1E showed that NMs consisted of nanofibers and Fe3O4 nanoparticles can be observed inside NMs. However, Ag-Fe3O4-NMs exhibited relatively denser surfaces after the immobilization of AgNPs (Fig. 1F), which may be due to the formation of AgNPs on the nanofibrous chitin surface. Meanwhile, the appearance of Fe and Ag element from Fe3O4-NMs and Ag-Fe3O4-NMs in EDX analysis (Fig. 1G and Fig. 1H) further indicated that Fe3O4 was embedded into NMs and AgNPs was successful immobilized into Fe3O4-NMs, respectively. The mesoporous structure of Fe3O4-NMs and Ag-Fe3O4-NMs can be seen from Fig. 1C and D, and also evaluated by BET analysis (Fig. 1I). A type IV isotherm curve with a loop was observed for Fe3O4-NMs and Ag-Fe3O4-NMs, suggesting that the two samples formed a well-defined 3D-porous structure (Duan et al., 2014). The surface area and pore size of Fe3O4-NMs were 182.4 m2/g and 16.4 nm, while those parameters of Ag-Fe3O4-NMs were 152.6 m2/g and 13.7 nm, respectively. The reason of smaller surface area and pore size for Ag-Fe3O4-NMs was owing to the immobilization of AgNPs on the nanofibrous chitin surface.

The magnetic properties of Fe3O4, Fe3O4-NMs, and Ag-Fe3O4-NMs were measured by VSM (Fig. 2 A). The saturation magnetization values of Fe3O4, Fe3O4-NMs, and Ag-Fe3O4-NMs were 53.45, 4.21, and 2.42 emµ/g, respectively. The separation of Ag-Fe3O4-NMs from solution can be observed in the inset of Fig. 3 C, which indicated the encapsulation of Fe3O4 into NMs. Fig. 2B shows the XRD patterns of chitin, Fe3O4-NMs, and Ag-Fe3O4-NMs. Obviously, Compared with chitin, five diffraction peaks at 2θ = 30.2° (2 2 0), 35.4° (3 1 1), 43.2° (4 0 0), 57.2° (5 1 1), and 62.7° (4 4 0) appeared in Fe3O4-NMs, which resulted from Fe3O4 nanoparticles. After the immobilization of AgNPs, the appearance of another four peaks at 38°, 44°, 61°, and 77° were assigned to the (1 1 1), (2 0 0), (2 2 0) and (3 1 1) planes, which indicated that a face centered cubic (fcc) lattice of the Ag crystal remained in the Fe3O4-NMs (Xiong et al., 2013). The XPS spectra of chitin, Fe3O4-NMs, and Ag-Fe3O4-NMs are shown in Fig. 2C, D, and E. Five peaks of Ag3d, Fe2p, C1s, O1s, and N1s elements appeared in Ag-Fe3O4-NMs (Fig. 2C), indicating that AgNPs were successfully immobilized into Fe3O4-NMs. As shown in Fig. 2D, it was clearly that two peaks at 711.4 and 724.5 eV were attributed to the Fe2p1/2 and Fe2p3/2 of Fe3O4. Fig. 2E showed that there were two individual peaks at 374.1 and 368.2 eV, which could be ascribed to the Ag3d5/2 and Ag3d3/2 electrons of metallic Ag, respectively. The binding energies of Ag3d5/2 were higher than that of Ag3d3/2, which may be due to the metallic Ag’s 3d states (Xu et al., 2017).

In biological systems, the surface wettability of materials is important for bacterial/cell adhesion, proliferation, and protein absorption on implants (Chen et al., 2018). Therefore, the swelling ratio and zeta potential of Fe3O4-NMs and Ag-Fe3O4-NMs were investigated, and measured the contact angle of Ag-Fe3O4-NMs were also measured. As shown in Fig. S2A, the swelling ratios of chitin, Fe3O4-NMs, and Ag-Fe3O4-NMs were 7.01, 5.45, and 5.06 g/g. The three materials could absorb more than 5 times their weight, which could be due to their high porosity and mechanical properties. The swelling ratio of Fe3O4-NMs and Ag-Fe3O4-NMs were lower than that of chitin, which could be due to the interaction between Fe3O4 or AgNPs and chitin nanofibers network, leading to a squeezed nanofibrous networks. As shown in Fig. S2B, the zeta potential of Fe3O4-NMs and Ag-Fe3O4-NMs was −0.97 ± 0.54 mV and −8.95 ± 1.56 mV, respectively. The notable reduction in the zeta potential of Ag-Fe3O4-NMs suggested an enhanced surface hydrophily after immobilization of AgNPs. The contact angle of Ag-Fe3O4-NMs is shown in Fig. S2C, and the measured water contact angle was approximately zero (100% wettability) after about 0.57 s. The superior hydrophilicity of the Ag-Fe3O4-NMs was mainly derived from the interconnecting pores of Ag-Fe3O4-NMs. All these properties demonstrated that Ag-Fe3O4-NMs have good wettability and suitable for wound dress engineering.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ag release from Ag-Fe3O4-NMs and Ag-Fe3O4-NMs + H2O2 in PBS
ce:section.ce:para: The Ag release rate was known as one of the most important parameter for evaluating silver-based antibacterial materials (Lu et al., 2017). It has been reported that Ag can effectively inhibit bacterial growth at release concentrations above 0.1 ppb (Xiong et al., 2013). In the present study, the release behavior of Ag from Ag-Fe3O4-NMs and Ag-Fe3O4-NMs + H2O2 in pH 7.0 solutions was analysed (Fig. 2F). The release amount of Ag from Ag-Fe3O4-NMs and Ag-Fe3O4-NMs + H2O2 on the first day was approximately 0.31 and 0.63 ppm, respectively, which was sufficient to inhibit bacterial growth. The release rate of Ag from Ag-Fe3O4-NMs and Ag-Fe3O4-NMs + H2O2 dramatically increased within 5 days, and it slowed down gradually with the immersion time at 5–21 days, suggesting that Ag-Fe3O4-NMs and Ag-Fe3O4-NMs + H2O2 had prolonged and steady release activity, which helped rapidly reach and maintain the effective concentration on the bacterial surface. The cumulative releases over 21 days for the Ag-Fe3O4-NMs and Ag-Fe3O4-NMs + H2O2 were approximately 3.4 and 5.3 ppm, respectively, which below the toxic concentration (10 ppm) (Liu et al., 2017). Obviously, the Ag release of Ag-Fe3O4-NMs + H2O2 were higher than that of Ag-Fe3O4-NMs, which was due to H2O2 can oxidation erosion AgNPs and destroy the connection between AgNPs with chitin. Moreover, sustained release of Ag could prevent AgNPs from aggregation on the surface of dead bacteria or in the media, which can improve the antibacterial effect.

The peroxidase-like activity of Ag-Fe3O4-NMs was investigated through catalytic oxidation of the peroxidase substrate TMB in the presence of H2O2 (Fig. 3). It can be seen that Ag-Fe3O4-NMs could catalyze the peroxidase substrate TMB to generate blue color in the presence of H2O2 (inset in Fig. 3A), which can be explained by the following reactions (1): Ag0 + 2H2O2 → Ag+ + O2
− + 2H2O; (2) Fe2+ + H2O2 → OH + Fe3+; (3) Fe3+ + H2O2 → O2
− + HO2
+ Fe2+ (Chen et al., 2014; Wei and Wang, 2013). Meanwhile, as show in Fig. 3B, it observed that the peroxidase-like activity was dependent on pH and temperature as well as other nanomaterial-based peroxidase mimics (Sun et al., 2014; Yin et al., 2016; Gao et al., 2014). As shown in Fig. 3C and Fig. 3D, a dramatic improvement of catalytic activity was observed with the steady concentration increasing of Ag-Fe3O4-NMs or H2O2. These results showed that AgNPs and Fe3O4 nanoparticles possessed intrinsic peroxidase-like catalytic activity, and which can effectively generate free radicals (OH) from H2O2 with higher efficiency. The catalytic mechanism was verified by steady-state kinetics theory and method. Fig. S3 showed the typical Michaelis-Menten curves of Ag-Fe3O4-NMs obtained in a certain range of H2O2 or TMB concentration. The Michaelis–Menten constant (Km) and the maximal reaction velocity (Vmax) were obtained by Lineweaver–Burk equation. As shown in Table S1, the Km values of Ag-Fe3O4-NMs with H2O2 as a substrate were slightly lower than that of horse radish peroxidase (HRP), which imply that the Ag-Fe3O4-NMs have higher affinity than that of HRP toward H2O2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial assessment of Ag-Fe3O4-NMs and synergistic antibacterial activity
ce:section.ce:para: To investigation the antibacterial capacity of Ag-Fe3O4-NMs and synergistic antibacterial activity of Ag-Fe3O4-NMs + H2O2, the antibacterial activities of Fe3O4, Fe3O4-NMs, and Ag-Fe3O4-NMs were qualitatively evaluated by measuring the viability of bacteria (colony counting). As shown in Fig. 4 A, there still remained turbid plates without any change in colonies for Fe3O4 and Fe3O4-NMs, indicating that the two samples have no antibacterial activity for E. coli and S. aureus. Interestingly, the results showed that Ag-Fe3O4-NMs completely killed E. coli and S. aureus colonies.

To further verify the antibacterial efficiency, a time and concentration kill assay was performed with Ag-Fe3O4-NMs at different intervals (15, 60, and 120 min) and concentrations (6.25, 12.5, 25, 50, and 100 μg/mL). Fig. 4B presented the percentage survival of E. coli and S. aureus after treatment with Ag-Fe3O4-NMs. For both strains, the antibacterial effects of Ag-Fe3O4-NMs showed a similar trend, that is; the bacterial reduction rapidly increased at first and then levelled off with aging time. The survival ratio of both strains loss gradually went up with the increasing concentration of Ag-Fe3O4-NMs, suggesting that the antibacterial activities of Ag-Fe3O4-NMs were concentration-dependent. As the concentration of Ag-Fe3O4-NMs increased, the antibacterial activities transformed from inhibition to killing action. However, the percentage survival of E. coli and S. aureus was different when treated with Ag-Fe3O4-NMs at the same concentration and incubation time. For E. coli, the percentage survival decreased to 49.2% when incubated with Ag-Fe3O4-NMs for 60 min at a concentration of 12.5 μg/mL. However, for S. aureus, approximately 40.0 μg/mL of Ag-Fe3O4-NMs were required to kill half of the bacteria for 60 min. The 100% loss in the viability of E. coli was fast-acting (60 min) when the concentration of Ag-Fe3O4-NMs achieved to 25.0 μg/mL, while that of S. aureus needed 50 μg/mL and 120 min. This difference was due to the fact that Gram-negative bacteria possessed only a thin peptidoglycan layer (approximately 2–3 nm) between the cytoplasmic and the outer membrane, while the gram-positive bacteria have a thick peptidoglycan layer of approximately 30 nm, indicating that the growth of S. aureus is not as easily inhibited as that of E. coli (Chen et al., 2015; Haider et al., 2016).

Because of the excellent peroxidase-like activity of Ag-Fe3O4-NMs and stronger antibacterial capacity of ·OH, the synergistic antibacterial system was investigated using a combination of Ag-Fe3O4-NMs (6.25 µg/mL) with H2O2 at different concentrations for 30 min. The results showed that the combination of Ag-Fe3O4-NMs with H2O2 has a stronger synergistic antibacterial activity in a dose-dependent manner (Fig. 4 C). For example, the survival rate was 51.95% and 73.78% for E. coli and S. aureus only in the presence of H2O2 (10−3 M), respectively. Additionally, Ag-Fe3O4-NMs (6.25 µg/mL) also exhibited antibacterial activity with a survival rate of 66.02% and 86.13% for E. coli and S. aureus without H2O2, respectively. Interestingly, the significant decreasing in bacterial growth was observed with the combination of Ag-Fe3O4-NMs (6.25 µg/mL) with H2O2 (10−3 M), the survival rate was only 1.19% and 40.62% for E. coli and S. aureus, respectively. Therefore, Ag-Fe3O4-NMs in this study presented a stronger synergistic antibacterial effect with a combination of H2O2, attributing that Ag-Fe3O4-NMs could serve as not only an antibacterial agent but also a nanocatalyst to enhance the activity of H2O2 by converting H2O2 into OH. The generated OH had a higher activity to oxidize nucleic acids, proteins, and polysaccharides in the matrix of biofilm (Gao et al., 2014).

The interaction between E. coli cell and Ag-Fe3O4-NMs was illustrated by determining the release of β-galactosidase from E. coli cytoplasm into culture medium using ONPG as the substrate. It has been reported that β-galactosidase is present in the cytoplasm, and which would be released after cytoplasmic membrane destroyed. The β-galactosidase can catalyze the ONPG hydrolysis to produce orthonitrophenol (ONP), which can be measured using UV–vis spectroscopy at 420 nm. As shown in Fig. S4, the release of β-galactosidase increased with increasing reaction time and concentration of Ag-Fe3O4-NMs, and reached a plateau after approximately 90 min, whereas no obvious change was measured for the control group. These results showed that Ag-Fe3O4-NMs have the capability to destroy the integrity of the cytoplasmic membrane and then release of β-galactosidase.

SEM and fluorescence microscopy were employed to further confirm the interaction between bacteria and antibacterial material. Fig. 5 A shows that the healthy and untreated bacteria of E. coli and S. aureus had a smooth and intact cell surface without any rupture (Fig. 5A a and c). After treatment with H2O2, a few disruptions were observed on the cell walls of E. coli and S. aureus, indicating that H2O2 has less of an influence on E. coli and S. aureus (Fig. 5A e and g). However, the rodlike shape of E. coli was not maintained after Ag-Fe3O4-NMs (Fig. 5A b) or Ag-Fe3O4-NMs + H2O2 (Fig. 5A f) treatments. Collapse and corrugation with a rugged surface was observed. Meanwhile, S. aureus morphology also changed significantly after treatment with Ag-Fe3O4-NMs + H2O2 (Fig. 5A h), showing a roughened and blebbed surface, with the complete destruction of some cells. The antibacterial ability of our materials was also investigated by fluorescence microscopy using the PI stain, which stains dead bacteria. Fig. 5B showed the fluorescence micrographs of E. coli and S. aureus treated with control, H2O2, Ag-Fe3O4-NMs, and Ag-Fe3O4-NMs + H2O2. The healthy and untreated bacteria of E. coli and S. aureus are shown in Fig. 5B a and d, which have no obvious fluorescence (Fig. 5B b and e), indicating that the cells were alive. After treated with H2O2, only a few red fluorescence spots were observed (Fig. 5B j and i). However, the bacterial cells fluoresced more red after Ag-Fe3O4-NMs (Fig. 5B c and f) or Ag-Fe3O4-NMs + H2O2 (Fig. 5B h and j) treatment. These results from SEM and fluorescence microscopy further suggested that Ag-Fe3O4-NMs + H2O2 can destroy bacterial surface structures and thus kill the bacteria.

Based on the results mentioned above, Ag-Fe3O4-NMs were found to exhibit a synergistic antibacterial effect when combined with H2O2. Four-step pathways may be the main reason for leading to the synergistic antibacterial activity (Fig. 6 ): (1) Ag-Fe3O4-NMs can easily capture and wrap negatively charged bacteria by electrostatic attraction. (2) There is a sustained release of Ag+ around the bacteria from Ag-Fe3O4-NMs. (3) AgNPs and Fe3O4 can efficiently catalyze decomposition H2O2 to generate a large amount of OH. (4) AgNPs, Ag+, and OH simultaneously display antibacterial activity against bacteria. AgNPs can exert physical damage to bacterial cells, penetrate the cytoplasm, and inactivate essential respiratory enzymes and proteins. Moreover, Ag+ can interact with the thiol groups of vital proteins, which result in the inactivation of enzyme proteins and disruption of respiration. Furthermore, ·OH can react with biofilms, lipids, proteins, and DNA, and disrupt these structures, causing bacterial death (Kittler et al., 2010; Yang et al., 2017). In this study, AgNPs were mostly immobilized on the surface of Fe3O4-NMs, and Fe3O4-NMs exhibited burst release and then followed with sustained release of Ag+. Thus, AgNPs, Ag+, and OH can rapidly reach the effective concentration for the burst release stage, and maintain an effective concentration over a long period at a sustained release stage, inducing long-term and enhanced synergistic antibacterial activity with a bio-safety dose for the combination of Ag-Fe3O4-NMs with H2O2.

As we all known, AgNPs have cellular toxicity by releasing silver ions, thus, it is necessary to evaluate the toxicity of Ag-Fe3O4-NMs. Fig. 7 A shows the cell viability of Fe3O4-NMs and Ag-Fe3O4-NMs at different concentrations ranging from 12.5 to 50 μg/mL after 24 h treatment. The cell viability increased from 96.53 to 117.17% when the concentration of Fe3O4-NMs increased from 12.5 to 50 μg/mL, suggesting that Fe3O4-NMs were safe for healthy cells due to the biocompatibility of chitin and bio-safety of Fe3O4 (Duan et al., 2015). Additionally, the cell viability reduced to 69.46% at 50 μg/mL for the Ag-Fe3O4-NMs, showing the increased toxicity of Ag-Fe3O4-NMs after increased dosage, which could be due to the sustained release of AgNPs from Ag-Fe3O4-NMs and aggregated on the cell surface (Chen et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2017). Meanwhile, To confirm the results of the MTT assay, the PI stain test was prepared as shown in Fig. 7B, after 24 h of incubation, the number of dead cells (red) have no obviously increased with concentration of Ag-Fe3O4-NMs enlargement, which revealed that good biocompatibility occurred in Ag-Fe3O4-NMs.

For further evaluation of the cytotoxicity of Fe3O4-NMs and Ag-Fe3O4-NMs, the hemolytic activity toward rabbit blood erythrocytes was carried out. The photographs of blood erythrocytes after exposure to the two samples for 2 h are shown in Fig. 7B. It was apparent that the hemoglobin release was not observed. Previous research reported that the permissible limit of hemolysis for blood containing biomaterial was 5.0% (Yang et al., 2017). From Fig. 7C, we can see that the hemolysis of Fe3O4-NMs was lower than 0.5% at different concentrations ranging from 12.5 to 50.0 μg/mL, whereas the hemolysis was lower than 4.0% for Ag-Fe3O4-NMs. These results further indicated that Ag-Fe3O4-NMs were relatively safe for healthy cells with good biocompatibility.

The potential of synergistic antibacterial activity for resisting wound infection in vivo was evaluated for the combination of Ag-Fe3O4-NMs with H2O2. Firstly, a wound disinfection model was built, and a type of dressing was designed (Fig. 8 B). Then, the infected models were divided into four groups: (Saline)-dressing (control group), (Ag-Fe3O4-NMs)-dressing group, (H2O2)-dressing group, and (Ag-Fe3O4-NMs + H2O2)-dressing group, respectively. The concentration of H2O2 used in test therapy was 1 mM, which was much lower than that commonly used (0.5–3%, ca. 166 mM to 1 M) (Miao et al., 2015). The wounds were photographed on days of 1, 4, 7, 11, and 15. During the therapeutic processes, the states of wound were different and results are shown in Fig. 8A. Severe inflammatory reaction with ulceration, erythema, and edema on the wounds for the control group was found on day 4, and the wound area was increased to 109.9 ± 13.21% (Fig. 8C). Interestingly, the (H2O2)-dressing group, (Ag-Fe3O4-NMs)-dressing group, and (Ag-Fe3O4-NMs + H2O2)-dressing group could effectively prevent wound infection and significantly accelerate wound healing after 4 days treatment, and the wound area was reduced to 94.3 ± 14.36%, 81.4 ± 12.33%, and 52.1 ± 10.17%, respectively. Notably, the wounds healed fastest healing with scab formation for the Ag-Fe3O4-NMs + H2O2 treatment group at day 4 (Fig. 8A and C). After 11 days of treatment, the wounds of the Ag-Fe3O4-NMs + H2O2 group were crusted compared to other groups. Meanwhile, the epithelialization was apparent for Ag-Fe3O4-NMs + H2O2 group on day 15. To define the host response to the infection, the wound exudates were collected on day 4, 7, and 11, and the antibacterial effect in vivo was quantified by determining the number of colony forming units (CFU). As shown in Fig. S5, the relative CFU count of the control group, H2O2 group, Ag-Fe3O4-NMs group, and Ag-Fe3O4-NMs + H2O2 group on day 4 was approximately 419.4 ± 174.92, 374.6 ± 177.18, 244.6 ± 67.67, and 194.8 ± 67.60 CFU/mL, respectively. After 10 days of treatment, the CFU count of the Ag-Fe3O4-NMs group and Ag-Fe3O4-NMs + H2O2 group decreased to 8.8 ± 7.91 and 6.2 ± 5.19 CFU/mL, respectively, whereas that of the control group and H2O2 group only decreased to 153 ± 23.84 and 106.8 ± 9.70 CFU/mL, respectively. These results indicated that the Ag-Fe3O4-NMs + H2O2 group had the strongest ability to kill bacteria compared to the other groups, particularly at the initial stage.

To further investigate the excellent wound-healing efficacy of the Ag-Fe3O4-NMs + H2O2 group, the histological analysis of wound skin staining with hematoxylin and eosin were evaluated after 7 and 15 days of treatment (Fig. 8D). For all groups on the days of 7, necrotic tissue was observed on the epidermis (indicated by black arrow) with the disappearance of the normal structure (dermis, fat layer, and muscle layer), which was replaced by the nascent granulation tissue (indicated by yellow arrow). We also observed that the connective tissue of the dermis became swollen and infiltrated by many inflammatory cells (indicated by red arrow) for all groups, indicating that the wounds have an inflammatory response after 7 days treatment. The results of Fig. 8D also showed that Ag-Fe3O4-NMs + H2O2 group had fewer inflammatory cells compared with other groups. On the other hand, the cell-free structure and focal hemorrhage were observed for the control group (indicated by blue arrow). After 15 days of treatment, the re-epithelialization (indicated by green arrow) and thicker granulation tissues (indicated by yellow arrow) were observed for all groups (Fig. 8D). The formation of re-epithelialization and granulation tissues was considered as the major steps for wound healing. However, the granulation tissues of control and H2O2 groups still have some inflammatory cells infiltration, showing that the two groups maintained inflammatory response (Lu et al., 2017; Chen et al., 2018). Meanwhile, the partial epidermal tissue of control and H2O2 groups were separated from the dermal tissue (indicated by red round). Excitingly, for the Ag-Fe3O4-NMs and Ag-Fe3O4-NMs + H2O2 groups, the granulation tissue has been organized to fibrous connective tissue, and the epidermis, dermis, and fat layer were clearly observed with new hair follicle structures (indicated by orange arrow) and abundant collagen content after 15 days of treatment. Furthermore, the new keratinocytes in the wounds treated with Ag-Fe3O4-NMs + H2O2 were much denser than those treated with Ag-Fe3O4-NMs (indicated by golden arrow). In addition, PCNA is selected as a cell proliferation marker to further investigate the underlying mechanism of the observed effects, and is detected by immunohistochemistry. As shown in the Fig. S6, It is indicated that using Ag-Fe3O4-NMs or combination of Ag-Fe3O4-NMs and H2O2 could promote PCNA expression more effectively than using H2O2 only. Finally, as shown in the Fig. S7, the histological analysis of rat organs does not been observed any abnormal effects after treatment for 15 days, indicating that the therapeutic process was safe. Therefore, the combination of Ag-Fe3O4-NMs with H2O2 at lower concentration has enhanced synergistic antibacterial activity, efficiently resisted wound infection, and further improved wound healing.

In summary, magnetic-nanofibrous microspheres (Fe3O4-NMs) were skilfully designed and successfully synthesized by chitin nanofibers self-assembly, and which was used to immobilization of AgNPs via coordination bonds. The well-dispersed distribution of AgNPs in the 3D-porous framework of Fe3O4-NMs could protect AgNPs from aggregation and allow the sustained release of nanoparticles from NMs vectors. Ag-Fe3O4-NMs exhibited remarkable and long-term antibacterial activity against gram-negatvie and gram-positive bacteria. Additionally, the intrinsic peroxidase-like activity of Ag-Fe3O4-NMs can catalyze the decomposition of H2O2 to generate ·OH at low concentrations. Moreover, the enhanced synergistic antibacterial effect was obtained at a bio-safety dose. The versatile synergy antibacterial system of Ag-Fe3O4-NMs and H2O2 could be expediently used in wound disinfection in vivo at a lower concentration. More importantly, the renewable natural resource and environmentally-friendly processes confer good biocompatibility on Ag-Fe3O4-NMs. Therefore, Ag-Fe3O4-NMs might be a promising and powerful weapon for combating the bacteria-induced infections.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Planning Subject of “the Twelfth Five-Yea-Plan” National Science and Technology for the Rural development of China (2013AA102203-05), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (31660482 and 21667018), and Outstanding Youth Foundation of Jiangxi, China (20171BCB23010).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijpharm.2018.10.002.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
